speaker: interviewer
text: --10, 1995, interview with Henry Coleman. You were saying that you had special papers that allowed you to travel within the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had a paper, you know, but after a while, the SS guards in the women's camp knew me already, because we were coming in and out every day. We had to come in several times, either to go for tools or lunchtime we had to bring in for the people. And the other, if they're-- we are divided-- we're actually about 25. We are divided like in four or five people in each camp. So we had to bring in food. Some-- some of the people from one of the group had to bring in food, soup for the people in the other camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I also had an opportunity to take some people who had, for instance, a sister or a mother in camp. I was able to take them in with me to see their sister, or mother, or brother, whatever. Because we had a-- a-- a magazine outside the camp, and we used to go in there several times a day to pick up different tools. So I told them if they worked in the area, in the-- in the area-- in the vicinity, I told them-- and they told me previously that I should look for so and so. And if I find them to let them know that they're-- they're alive. And then I took them back and they met, which was a big thing, because you couldn't go-- a man could not go into-- to a woman's camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you even saw-- if an SS woman saw you talking to a woman, you were with a-- with a whip, she whipped you very badly. I got whipped a few times already from a-- until they got to know me. And they saw me, even though I worked inside, I was not allowed to talk to women. But, you know, when you went inside, you couldn't help sometimes so you spoke a word or two. And-- and you didn't see if she was in back of you when she saw you talking, she whipped you. They were very-- they were even worse than the men, the-- the woman SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met also my first wife in camp, because I knew my first wife. Because my-- she was engaged to my middle brother and I met the woman when they shipped us from Mlawa to [INAUDIBLE]. And then we came to live with where my brother was, so I knew my-- he was engaged to my first wife. So I met her there, because I was told by other women that she's there. And I helped her from time to time if I could. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I used to go, and like I said, from camp to camp, but then that stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when they took us to work outside the town of Auschwitz, we use to renovate houses for Germans who came from Germany to live there. I don't know if they were part of the administration of the Auschwitz camp or not. I don't know. But we were renovating houses for them. And we used to march every day about five kilometers from camp. And we had five guards, SS with dogs, with those shepherd-- German shepherds. They used to take us every day to work and back from work. Now we also work for the same company for [? Nebel. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a while, I got to know the civilian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Germans who were ahead-- were at the head of the company, or the head of the section. And, you know, they were always asking me if I have any gold, or dollars, or diamonds. And I had-- I had access to it because of the Sonderkommando. They had access to all that thing, because they were sorting out all that money, the diamonds, everything. You know, they had access to everything. So they were taken always with them to their quarters some, you know, because there was no accounting of what they found or what they had selected. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to get from them, and I used to give it to these Germans. And they gave me-- they gave-- brought bread, cigarettes I smoked them also. Cigarettes and sometimes salami.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the problem was to take it into camp, because when you walked into camp every day, every evening after work, there were a row of SS standing on each side and watching if you have no any-- no bulges, because stomachs nobody had, you know. Because from the food they give you, couldn't have no stomach. So we used to hide it in our pants, tied down the pants or in the pants, you know, no big package, always small packages. One day I remember they were going through very thoroughly, you know, and I had on me some stuff. And I knew they-- they gonna find it on me, because they were close to me. And I ran away at that time, too. But the-- the-- my luck was that a lot of people were already on that street who came back from work. So I was mixed in with them, so I had a chance to run into my block, to my barrack, empty it out, and I just walk out and that's it. Well, anyway I was lucky. From then on, in fact I had a case once while on the job. That was already in 1944 where the Russians-- where the Russians were already pushing the Germans back, where they lost the Stalingrad war, you know, battle. And somehow these SS, we had the head of the SS, [INAUDIBLE] four SS. We had one SS head, which was an oberscharfuhrer. He was a Dutch German. And I don't know if they were turning a new leaf or not, but he became all of a sudden very friendly, you know. And he said to me already, you know, if I can get gold, or diamonds, or gold. And I wouldn't take a chance with him, you know. But one evening, they got all drunk, the SS guards and all the Polacks, too. They were all drunk. And, you know, we have to go back to camp and you cannot go back to camp drunk, because we-- they put us [INAUDIBLE]. They are like [INAUDIBLE] kommando. There was like one group. If they-- if you, you know, they, you know, committed some kind of infraction or-- or crime, they put you in that kommando and you know you were finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was carrying four rifles and four dogs and on leashes. And we were going with back roads to camp till they sobered out a little bit and we went into camp. But this-- there was-- this-- this incident happened only once.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, you know, I couldn't take anymore, because the kapo which we had was a Polack from-- Marouta was his name. Marouta was like a butcher. When you-- we used to walk from camp to that-- to that place. We had to walk like soldiers. If you skipped the foot, you know, like right left, right left. If you skipped the-- the foot, he beated you. He kicked you terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided, you know, I had a chance to switch to another construction company by the name [? Anhalt, ?] also a German company. And we used to do all the inside work in the camps again, in all the camps. I worked in the Hungarian women's camp, in the other women's camp, in the-- there was a gypsy camp also. The gypsy camp was right next to our camp where I think by September 1944 they told us we cannot walk out after roll call.

speaker: interviewee
text: We already knew that they're emptying out the gypsy camp and they killed them all. And I worked in there. There was so many children there, you know, nice girls, and women, and men. They gassed them all. They put them all into the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I worked for that company [? Anhalt. ?] And my kapo, who was a German, was very nice also. He never hurt anybody, you know, and he was very pleasant. He used to let us go and talk to-- to women if we knew somebody. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked there till about the end of September. Then they came in, and they picked from each barrack young people. And they put us into a separate barrack. And they told us to undress, and we were sitting on the beds naked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said-- somebody said they-- they're shipping us to coal mines, [INAUDIBLE] which was near Buna, where they shipped a lot of Hungarian people where they died. A lot of them died in these coal mines. And I-- I let out work. You know, I knew somebody in camp, a Polish guy. He was from Zamosc, which is a town in Poland near Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there, a prominent one. And he had-- his number was 7638. He was there from the first ones. And he was an architect, but he had to some kind-- a lot of connections there in camp. So he knew about me that I was put into [? the ?] barrack. And he knew somebody in the main chancellery, there in camp, and somehow he got me out from that barrack. I was-- I was put back into-- my own block. And actually all the people from there, I don't know where they were. If they were killed or shipped to-- to coal mines, I don't know, because it was very hard to know. News didn't travel very fast there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In October 1944, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they put us into the empty-- where the gypsies were. They put a-- about 700, 800 people, young people mostly. And they kept us there for a couple days, and then they took us to the railroad. And we went on the railroad. We came to Stutthof, which is another [? bread ?] camp. Was-- of course we heard a lot of stories there, you know. They brought them from Lithuania, from Latvia, Estonia, a lot of Jewish people and women by a ship and they drowned them in the Baltic Sea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in Stutthof for about two, three weeks, and it was also very bad. They gave very little food. We didn't do anything. We didn't do any work. After three weeks, they put us again on boats and we thought that they were going to drown us in the Baltic.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they took us to a small camp outside of Danzig. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember the camp. I don't remember the name. I don't even know if the camp-- the name-- the camp had a name or not. But it was a small camp, and it had a wire fence. We were on one side and the SS was on the other side of the fence, and they had some [? dogs ?] there. We had mostly Jews from Kovno, from Lithuania, from Latvia. We were only a few of a small group from Auschwitz-Birkenau, maybe 50, 60, a few that I very close with. And the-- and the head of the camp was a Latvian Jew or a Lithuanian Jew. He was a young man, a tall man, very nice looking man, but he was like a butcher. He was beating and kicking people unbelievable. And then there was another man from Auschwitz, which was already a couple in Auschwitz. He was from a town near [INAUDIBLE], Plonsk. But he was from the-- from before the war-- before the war, I heard from the underworld, he was like-- like a criminal from before the war. He was a Jew, but he was a criminal. And he was his assistant, that-- that head of the camp. And the two of them were a good team, because they were beating people terrible for any little infraction. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came to that camp, the next day they took us to Danzig to the port area to a-- a-- an island. It's called Holm, where all of ships there, battleships and submarines. And they put us up in big, like, warehouses. And they taught us how to weld plates. So we worked. Some weeks I worked nights. Some weeks I worked days. So we worked both shifts. After like three weeks, they put us up on the ships to do some welding. I-- I knew welding like I know-- after three weeks I knew welding like I know dentistry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, but I-- I knew already. I held the mask and I hold that I thing. I know how to the distance to hold from the plate. And they put us up. I remember they put us up once in a big-- the ship was [INAUDIBLE] on the-- on the edge of the, you know, the water. And there was a ladder put up on top of the ship. And we had to climb up, you know, that big ladder into that battleship. So I was with another man. I mentioned before I was not afraid of anything. I was like fearless. And I came up almost on the top of the ladder, and he was down at almost in the middle. When you came up to the middle of the ladder, you were over the water and he wouldn't go any further. So he went down and [? he can-- ?] they beat-- they were beating him, kicking him, but he [INAUDIBLE]. When I was up there myself, and I was welding there, not a very good job but I was welding. So I was there-- we were there a few-- few weeks. But again, there was very little food. The Germans themselves didn't have any food. One night, we knew the next morning they supposed to evacuate us, because they were already bombarding Danzig. We couldn't go into work anymore, because they were bombarding it in the evening from planes and we heard artillery. You know. We saw the Katyushas already, the Russian Katyushas. So we couldn't-- they didn't take us to work anymore. After a few days or maybe a week, they-- they said the next morning we were being evacuated. Now they didn't give us any food, anything, nothing. They were going to put us on the road without any food. We knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So myself and another friend of mine, we decided we're going to break in at night to the German barracks there where they kept their food. And there was a guard going back and forth. There was snow already on the ground. So we climbed over the-- underneath the fence. He was on the other side. I was the only one climbing on the other side. I waited till the guard was going to the end of the-- to the barracks. There was like a row of barracks about 20 feet long. I already had a-- a-- I made a key from a--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was working at the welding place, I made a key, a pass key like it's called. I don't know how it's called in English. I forgot, but I know how we call it in Jewish. It's called a [YIDDISH] Anyway, I opened that thing because I tried. They took us sometimes to work there. So I tried that thing at the door and I went in there. And I took out-- they didn't have too much food, you know. I took a little bit and I threw it over the fence from the other side of the window into the snow. And the guy, my guy went into the barrack, and we had to be very careful from our own people too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we buried it in a-- into a chimney, we like dug out the-- in between the bricks, the cement. We took out a couple bricks and we put it into the-- into the chimney. And the next morning they turned over the whole campus looking for the food, because they were so limited food already. They were beating people [INAUDIBLE] but nobody knew. So anyway, we-- they put us up on a march. We were walking for a few days without any food. Some people were dying, you know, because they were falling back and they were shooting [INAUDIBLE]. One man, I remember an older man I was carrying. We were like six guys which we were kept together. So we made up-- you know, we made up our mind, you know. Whoever's going to stay behind, we're going to bring them to the front, and we're going to go back and forth. But one man I remember was an older man. I carried him literally on my shoulder, because he couldn't walk anymore. To the front a few times, but then at the end, he was shot. An old German SS shot him. And they let him lay there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the same evening, we came to a farm. And there were American prisoners of war in that farm already, too. And that guard took me and another friend of mine with a horse and wagon with the American soldier was-- was driving the-- I mean, he was, you know, leading the horses to bury, which was-- I was very surprised to bury that old man.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was already late in February or beginning of March. And we went up. It was like hills, and we went up with picks. And we-- the-- the ground was frozen, and we dug up a grave. And we said Kaddish, and we buried him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then on our way back, he tried to justify himself that he was old. He was sick. He couldn't make it. So I said to myself, God, give me a chance so I can get back to him and take revenge on him. And we-- we stayed there overnight and the next morning we were on the march again. We came to another place. They put us up in a church, but we had a SS-- the women-- we were together with women. And the women had a separate SS, like a higher officer. And we had like an oberscharfuhrer, which was like a sergeant in the SS. He was a tall man, an older man. He was traveling on a bicycle. We came and-- and you know, he was very nice, being way the SS were, he was very nice. He came ahead of us, you know, and he went into-- to that town, that village. Not a town, a village. And he got us soup, a little bit of soup for each one. And we slept in the church and then we traveled again the next morning. We marched again. That was already March 8. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And March 8 we came to a place. On one side was a forest. On the other side was a big barn. There was a-- a wooden barn, a long one. And on the side of the barn were like from stoneware also up on the barn or whatever. Now we stood there for a while and they were debating our-- our SS and the SS on the women. And we already thought they're going to bring us into the forest and shoot us all, but they were-- we were like 700, 800 people, and they were only like 20 guards, or maybe 25, 30 guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got together, and we decided being in that last camp, where we already-- we already couldn't go to work in Danzig. They took us once to a railroad derailed, some wagons derailed. And they put us there to put the wagons back on the rails. They were all-- the wagons were all filled with-- with packages and suitcases, Germans who were going back to Germany already, because they were afraid that the Russians-- the Russians, you know, coming back, coming in. So they had a lot of suitcases, and you know, a lot of like people leaving homes. We cut open every suitcase, every bag, and we found-- we found some-- some food, but we couldn't take too much with us because it would be obvious. And we found revolvers and bullets. So we divided between us like four or five revolvers. So we were hiding it. We found ammunition in the-- in the suitcases.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came to that place, to that forest, we got together, the four of us, because it was dark already. It was raining, like drizzling. And they couldn't see us whether we're moving or not. And we decided if they-- if we go and start to go into the forest, we start shooting, and we let a few people know to run. But it so happened they put us all into the barn. About 5 o'clock in the morning, we heard a lot of noise. And we look out the barn, and there were five tanks with the thing pointing at the barn, the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guns, yeah, pointing at the barn. And I never seen Russians, and I never knew Russian. But the women were there from the Lithuanian area, they-- they already experienced the Russian. They were under Russian rule already, so they knew the Russians. So they start screaming, Russians, Russians! And everybody's running out from the barn and the Russians sitting there on the tanks. The people who could converse with them spoke to them, and they were told to quickly move back. And there were a lot of horses and wagons. Germans who were running away from the Russians, they left their horses and wagons there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were told or we were told to grab whatever we can, any-- any horses and wagons and quickly move back, because they're only five tanks. They can-- they can be pushed back by the Germans. Move back as far as we can into-- into town or whatever. So we grabbed a wagon. About-- yeah, before-- before I say that, as we're grabbing the wagons, I take a look and I see a familiar face. You know, that German who shot that Jewish man was already in civilian clothes. The Russians, the-- the-- the SS that the Russians found, they shot them all. They put them out in like a circle, and they shot them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow he must have got-- he escaped from there, and I take a look. He's in a civilian-- already in civilian clothes. And I-- you know, I had the revolver on me, and I shot him in the back maybe five times. He was already going up on a wagon with his right foot on top. You know. The wagon have a step to go up, and I shot him right in the back five times, and he like kicked over and he fell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, an older Jewish man-- you know, I don't know but our Jewish people have very short memories. You know. And he said to me, you know, why-- why did I shoot him. And I said, well, what do you mean why? I said, he-- he shot a man, you know, for no reason at all. He was already a few days before the liberation, you know, and there's no reason you had to shot him. It so happened that, you know, somehow he must've given him money or what and he was trying to protect him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we were on the wagons. You know, we were traveling for-- for about 10 days. And we already had-- there were 1, 2, 3, three girls and an older woman, Hungarian. And there were like six or seven men. We were on the wagon. There were a few. Most of them were from Auschwitz, you know, which I know. And we didn't know the area, so we traveled around like in back roads and forests. And after two-- 10 days, we came back to the same spot that we left because they wouldn't let us travel with the-- with the roads. Because there were a lot of tanks coming in, the Russian columns with tanks and tanks and soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we met a lot of Russians on the road. They want to take away our horses, you know, and we heard a lot of cases where the Russians raped the women. You know, they took the women and young men to work in their quarters, you know. Did you see any of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally didn't see, but I heard from other people, yeah. And we came-- we came to a house, to a German house. I was traveling on a bicycle already, because I always traveled on a bicycle. So I loved the bicycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I grabbed a bicycle from a German, and they were on a wagon. And I was traveling like a-- I was a scout. Because I-- we were told to be very careful, because there were a lot of Germans running around with-- with rifles and shooting Jews whenever they see. They knew, because we wore the uniforms. You know? So I was on a-- traveling on a bicycle, and when it got-- when it got dark, or it's like dawn, you know, I was looking for quarters where we can stay overnight. I came to a house, and I found a house by the road. And I came in, and I told the Germans-- I had already now not a gun, but I had a machine gun. You know. And I had like a belt here with bullets. Where did you get that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, we found-- we found a lot of wagons with ammunition, you know. We were looking for ammunition, for a lot of things. We found cigarettes. You know, we found food, a lot of things [? where ?] German, you know, left. You know, they ran away. You know, they left everything standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I walked into the door, you know, and they saw me. But I wore my-- my-- my hat, which I gave way to a museum. I still have my uniform, my hat from Auschwitz. And I stand in the door, and I-- I point my gun like this. And I start shooting, but not at the people. There were no men. They were old women, and old men, and children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I don't have the heart, because I said to myself when I was in Auschwitz, we were sometimes sitting. We-- we gave us like 15, 20 minutes, you know, reprieve, you know, to eat our soup. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I promised myself, you know, I says, as soon as I come out, I will shoot any German kid, woman, old, young, anyone. I'll just kill. But you know, a Jewish heart, how can you do that? So I stand there in the door, and I start shooting, and I shot into a cuckoo clock. And I said to them, I'll give you 20 minutes to leave the house. And they start crying. It was like drizzling already, too. It was March, always drizzling in Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't care. There's a barn right next-- there was a barn right next door. I said, I don't care. You go into the barn. I says, I do more for you than you Germans did for me or for my family. And I said, all right. [INAUDIBLE] they want to take their blankets. I say, well take your blankets and go, and they all left the house. And then I went up to the road, and I pointed my friends with the horse and wagon, and we came in. And they already had dinner on the table. That was the big mistake we made, you know. And we sat down and we ate. It was meat and other things. And most of us got sick after that dinner. And then Russians came in already. I remember one Russian came in with a-- with a-- not an accordion, but those small things that they have, like a [RUSSIAN] they call. And he brought in a bottle of vodka, you know, and we were not used to-- to-- to drink that stuff and eat that stuff, you know. But they stayed with us there. They stayed with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: They stayed with us for a few minutes and then they left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was near Lowenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Lowenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In East Prussia, in the area of Konigsberg
